title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia
authors: Jennifer A. Woyach, John C. Byrd

items.title: Incidence and Demographics of CLL
items.content: CLL predominantly affects older adults, with a median diagnosis age of 71. The incidence in the U.S. is 4.6 per 100,000 people annually. It has a male-to-female ratio of 2:1, which equalizes after age 80. CLL is most common among Caucasians and rare in Asians.
keyPoints: Median diagnosis age: 71
Age-adjusted incidence: 4.6/100,000 people annually
Male-to-female ratio: 2:1, equalizes after age 80
Most common in Caucasians; rare in Asians
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Survival Trends and Familial Risk of CLL
items.content: The overall 5-year survival for CLL has improved from 70% in 1980 to over 92% by 2015. First-degree relatives have an 8.5-fold increased risk of developing CLL, with MBL more common in families with multiple affected members.
keyPoints: 5-year overall survival: Increased from 70% (1980) to >92% (2015)
First-degree relative risk: 8.5-fold increase
MBL prevalence higher in families with two first-degree CLL cases
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Genetic and Environmental Factors in CLL
items.content: CLL is not linked to radiation exposure but associated with Agent Orange, burn pit, and Camp Lejeune water exposures. Genetic predisposition is supported by GWAS identifying ~30 SNPs accounting for 19% of familial risk.
keyPoints: No definitive link to radiation
Associated with: Agent Orange, burn pits, Camp Lejeune water
~30 SNPs identified in GWAS; account for 19% of familial risk
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL
items.title: Understanding the Role of IGHV Mutational Status in CLL Prognosis
items.content: The mutational status of the immunoglobulin heavy chain variable (IGHV) region is a crucial prognostic factor in chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). Approximately 60% of patients have IGHV that is ≥2% mutated from germline, which typically indicates a more mature B-cell origin and is associated with an indolent disease course. Conversely, about 40% of patients exhibit IGHV <2% mutation, correlating with rapid disease progression and shorter survival.
keyPoints: IGHV ≥2% mutated from germline in ~60% of CLL patients indicates a more mature B-cell origin and is associated with an indolent course.
IGHV <2% mutated from germline in ~40% of patients correlates with rapid progression and shorter survival pre-therapeutics targeting BCR.

Unfavorable biological properties such as enhanced telomerase activity, overexpression of activation-induced cytidine deaminase, increased NF-κB activity, and high-risk genomic mutations (e.g., NOTCH1, SF3B1, TP53, ATM) are linked with IGHV unmutated disease.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL

items.subtopic: CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL
items.title: The Significance of Stereotyped BCRs in Predicting CLL Clinical Course
items.content: Approximately 30% of CLL patients express stereotyped B-cell receptors (BCRs), which are associated with specific clinical outcomes. Certain stereotype subsets, such as subsets 1 and 2, predict a higher-risk disease course.
keyPoints: About 30% of CLL patients have stereotyped BCRs.
Stereotype subsets 1 and 2 are predictive of a higher-risk clinical course in CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS
items.title: Prognostic Impact of Cytogenetic Abnormalities in CLL
items.content: Cytogenetic abnormalities are crucial prognostic factors in chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), identified primarily through FISH analysis. Key abnormalities include del(13)(q14.3), trisomy 12, del(11)(q22.3), and del(17)(p13.1). Del(13)(q14.3) is linked to indolent disease and good therapy response, while del(17)(p13.1) indicates poor prognosis due to TP53 loss.
keyPoints: del(13)(q14.3) associated with more indolent disease and prolonged survival
Trisomy 12 has an intermediate prognosis
del(11)(q22.3) linked to aggressive disease, especially in young patients
del(17)(p13.1) results in loss of TP53, poorest prognosis, rapid progression
Complex karyotype (three or more abnormalities) significantly worsens time to treatment and survival
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS
items.title: Role of Gene Mutations in CLL Prognosis and Treatment Response
items.content: Gene mutations such as NOTCH1, SF3B1, MYD88, ATM, and TP53 significantly influence CLL prognosis and treatment response. NOTCH1 mutations (~15%) are linked to trisomy 12 and reduced CD20 therapy sensitivity. TP53 mutations (~5% in early-stage) coexist with del(17)(p13.1), leading to poor outcomes.
keyPoints: NOTCH1 mutations (~15%), often associated with trisomy 12, linked to lower CD20 therapy response
SF3B1 mutations (10-15%) correlate with intermediate-risk disease
TP53 mutations found in ~5% of early-stage CLL and up to 40% in later stages; coexist with del(17)(p13.1) 70% of the time, leading to poor prognosis
ATM mutations (~10-15%), often coexisting with del(11)(q22.3), impair DNA damage response
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS
items.title: MicroRNA Alterations in CLL Pathogenesis
items.content: Noncoding genes, particularly microRNAs (miRs), play a significant role in CLL. The most common cytogenetic abnormality, del(13)(q14.3), leads to loss of the miR15/16 cluster, crucial for inhibiting antiapoptotic gene expression and thus promoting cell survival.
keyPoints: del(13)(q14.3) results in loss of miR15/16 cluster, leading to overexpression of antiapoptotic genes like BCL2
Loss of miR181a leads to increased MCL1 and TCL1 expression
Overexpression of miR-155, an onco-miR, is common in CLL patients
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS

items.title: How T-cell Dysfunction Contributes to CLL Pathogenesis
items.content: In chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), the adaptive immune system, particularly CD4+ T-cells, exhibits qualitative defects due to chronic antigen stimulation. This leads to a shift from a Th1 cytotoxic phenotype to a Th2 immunosuppressive phenotype, impairing T-cell cytotoxic capacity and promoting tumor cell tolerance.
keyPoints: CD4+ T-cell compartment shows qualitative defects similar to chronic viral infections.
Shift from Th1 to Th2 phenotype leads to expansion of immunosuppressive cytokines like IL-10.
~85% of patients experience hypogammaglobulinemia affecting all immunoglobulin subclasses during their disease course.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · IMMUNOLOGY

items.title: Role of Hypogammaglobulinemia in CLL and Infection Risk
items.content: Hypogammaglobulinemia is prevalent in CLL, affecting ~85% of patients at some point. It involves all immunoglobulin subclasses and correlates with increased infection risk, particularly when IgG and IgA levels are low.
keyPoints: Hypogammaglobulinemia affects ~85% of CLL patients over their disease course.
Occurs in all immunoglobulin subclasses.
Low IgG and IgA correlate with higher infection risk; isolated IgM reduction does not increase infection risk.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · IMMUNOLOGY

items.title: Diagnosis of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) via Flow Cytometry
items.content: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia is often diagnosed incidentally during routine blood work, which shows an elevated lymphocyte count. The definitive diagnosis involves flow cytometry to identify the characteristic immunophenotype of CLL cells.
keyPoints: CLL typically presents with an incidental finding on routine blood tests showing elevated lymphocytes.
Flow cytometry is essential for confirming CLL by identifying B-cell markers CD19, CD20, CD22, CD23, CD200, and the T-cell marker CD5 along with dim surface immunoglobulin (kappa or lambda).
A clonal B-cell count of ≥ 5 × 10^9/L on flow cytometry confirms CLL without further workup.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Differentiating Small Lymphocytic Lymphoma (SLL) from CLL
items.content: In cases where patients have lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly with a small clonal proliferation of CLL cells in the blood, the diagnosis is often SLL. Despite different presentations, SLL and CLL share identical genetic and molecular features and are managed similarly.
keyPoints: SLL presents with lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly alongside a small clonal B-cell count in peripheral blood.
Genetic and molecular characteristics of SLL are the same as those of CLL.
Management for SLL is identical to that of CLL, although patients may later develop bone marrow involvement.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Understanding MBL as a Precursor to CLL
items.content: Monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis (MBL) is recognized as a precursor to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), characterized by the presence of clonal B cells in peripheral blood without meeting full CLL criteria. It's important for prognosis and management, distinguishing between low-count (<0.5 × 10^9/L) and high-count (>0.5 × 10^9 but <5 × 10^9/L) MBL.
keyPoints: MBL is a precursor to CLL with clonal B cells in peripheral blood without full CLL criteria.
Low-count MBL has a negligible progression rate to CLL, while high-count progresses at 1–2% per year.
Prevalence of MBL is up to ~12% in the general population, more common in elderly men and first-degree relatives of CLL patients (~18%).
MBL patients have increased rates of serious infections and secondary cancers, suggesting immune dysfunction similar to CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · MONOCLONAL B-CELL LYMPHOCYTOSIS

items.title: Monitoring Strategies for MBL Patients
items.content: Given the low risk of progression from MBL to CLL but increased complications like infections and secondary cancers, monitoring focuses on vaccinations and cancer screening. Follow-up is typically managed by primary care physicians unless progression to CLL occurs.
keyPoints: MBL patients have a 16% chance of hospitalization over four years due to serious infections, similar to newly diagnosed CLL (18.4%).
Monitoring should emphasize vaccinations and age-appropriate cancer screening due to higher complication rates than progression risk.
Follow-up for MBL is generally managed by primary care physicians, unlike CLL which involves co-management with hematologists.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · MONOCLONAL B-CELL LYMPHOCYTOSIS

items.title: Infections as Leading Cause of Morbidity and Mortality in CLL Patients
items.content: Patients with Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) are at a heightened risk for infections due to immune dysfunction inherent to the disease. This complication is significant, contributing to approximately 30-50% of deaths in these patients.
keyPoints: Infections account for ~30–50% of deaths in CLL patients.
Patients with CLL have an increased risk for both typical and atypical infections due to immune dysfunction.
Viral prophylaxis is recommended when initiating therapy, particularly for those with a history of varicella-zoster.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · COMPLICATIONS OF CLL | INFECTIONS

items.title: Management of Hypogammaglobulinemia and Infection Risk in CLL Patients
items.content: Hypogammaglobulinemia is common in CLL, increasing the risk for infections. Prophylactic intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) can reduce minor or moderate bacterial infections, particularly in patients with recurrent infections or pulmonary bronchiectasis.
keyPoints: Hypogammaglobulinemia is associated with increased infection risk, especially mucocutaneous and urinary tract infections.
Prophylactic IVIg reduces the number of minor or moderate bacterial infections but does not improve survival.
IVIg is indicated for patients with recurrent infections, pulmonary bronchiectasis, serious hospitalizations due to infection, or IgG levels <300–500 mg/dL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · COMPLICATIONS OF CLL | INFECTIONS

items.title: Increased Risk and Management of Secondary Malignancies in CLL Patients
items.content: Patients with Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) have a significantly elevated risk for developing secondary malignancies, particularly skin cancers. This risk is up to three times higher than the general population, even without cytotoxic chemotherapy. Skin cancer incidence can be 8- to 15-fold higher and may exhibit more aggressive behavior. Factors such as older age at diagnosis, male sex, high $ \beta_{2} $-microglobulin, elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and chronic kidney disease are associated with this increased risk.
keyPoints: Risk of secondary malignancies in CLL patients is up to three times higher than the general population.
Skin cancers are particularly common, occurring 8- to 15-fold more frequently.
Factors increasing cancer risk include older age at diagnosis, male sex, high $ \beta_{2} $-microglobulin, elevated LDH, and chronic kidney disease.
No specific increased screening recommendations; however, age- and sex-appropriate screenings are advised, with extended screening beyond 70–75 years for CLL patients.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · SECONDARY MALIGNANCIES | AUTOIMMUNE COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Autoimmune Complications in CLL: Diagnosis and Management of AIHA
items.content: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) is a common autoimmune complication in CLL, characterized by antibody-mediated destruction of red blood cells. It presents with isolated anemia, elevated reticulocyte count, and signs of hemolysis such as increased bilirubin and LDH levels, along with low haptoglobin. Diagnosis can be confirmed with the detection of warm IgG antibodies on RBCs using a Coombs test.
keyPoints: AIHA is common in CLL, presenting with isolated anemia and signs of hemolysis.
Key diagnostic features include elevated reticulocyte count, bilirubin, LDH, and low haptoglobin.
Coombs test detects warm IgG antibodies on RBCs to confirm AIHA diagnosis.
Immediate therapy often involves glucocorticoids; rituximab may be used for resistant cases.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · SECONDARY MALIGNANCIES | AUTOIMMUNE COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Approach to Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP) in CLL Patients
items.content: Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) is another frequent autoimmune complication in CLL, often presenting as isolated thrombocytopenia. Diagnosis can be challenging but may involve observing a rapid decline in platelet levels without an alternative cause. Treatment typically begins when platelet counts drop to 20,000–30,000 or if there are bleeding complications.
keyPoints: ITP is common in CLL and presents as isolated thrombocytopenia.
Diagnosis involves identifying a rapid decline in platelets without alternative causes.
Treatment starts when platelet counts fall to 20,000–30,000 or if there are bleeding issues.
Initial treatment includes glucocorticoids and IVIg; rituximab can induce long-term remissions.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · SECONDARY MALIGNANCIES | AUTOIMMUNE COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Recognizing and Diagnosing Richter's Transformation in CLL
items.content: Richter's transformation is a critical complication of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) where it transforms into an aggressive lymphoma, most commonly Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma (DLBCL). Identifying this transformation involves recognizing clinical signs such as rapid adenopathy progression and constitutional symptoms. Diagnostic imaging with ^{18}FDG-PET/CT is essential, using SUVs to differentiate between CLL and Richter's transformation.
keyPoints: Richter's transformation transforms CLL into an aggressive lymphoma, most commonly DLBCL.
Prevalence: 0.5% per year for DLBCL, 0.05% per year for HL.
Risk factors include bulky lymphadenopathy, NOTCH1 mutations, del(17)(p13.1), and specific stereotyped IGVH usage.
Clinical signs: rapid adenopathy progression, fatigue, night sweats, fever, weight loss; LDH is usually high.
^{18}FDG-PET/CT scan with SUVs <5 suggests CLL; SUVs >5 are suspicious for Richter's transformation; SUVs ≥10 are very concerning.
Preferred diagnostic method: excisional biopsy.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · RICHTER'S TRANSFORMATION

items.title: Therapeutic Approaches and Prognosis in Richter's Transformation
items.content: Treatment of DLBCL arising from CLL involves combination chemoimmunotherapy, though outcomes are generally poor. Median survival is 6–16 months for clonally related transformations versus ~5 years for unrelated ones. For fit patients responding to initial therapy, stem cell transplantation may induce long-term remissions. Emerging therapies like CAR-T and bispecific antibodies show promise but require further investigation.
keyPoints: Therapy typically involves combination chemoimmunotherapy with poor outcomes; median survival 6–16 months for clonally related Richter's transformation.
Clonally unrelated transformations have a better prognosis, with ~5 years median survival.
Stem cell transplantation may induce long-term remissions in responsive patients.
CAR-T therapy and bispecific antibodies are promising but under investigation.
Hodgkin's disease transformation treated according to standard Hodgkin's protocols.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · RICHTER'S TRANSFORMATION

items.title: When to Perform Prognostic Testing in CLL
items.content: Prognostic testing using FISH, stimulated karyotype, and sequencing for TP53 and IGHV mutation status can be performed if patients desire information on the expected natural history of their CLL.
keyPoints: FISH and stimulated karyotype are used for prognostic testing in CLL.
Sequencing for TP53 and IGHV mutations provides additional prognostic information.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · WORKUP AND STAGING | STAGING

items.title: Imaging and Bone Marrow Biopsy Considerations in CLL
items.content: CT imaging is generally not required unless there are symptoms suggesting intraabdominal nodes out of proportion to peripheral nodes. Bone marrow biopsy is typically deferred until therapy initiation, except for unexplained cytopenias.
keyPoints: CT scan is not necessary without specific symptoms indicating disproportionate intraabdominal node involvement.
Bone marrow biopsy is usually postponed until treatment unless there are unexplained cytopenias.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · WORKUP AND STAGING | STAGING

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)
items.title: When to Initiate Therapy in CLL: Targeted Therapies vs. Chemoimmunotherapy
items.content: The initiation of therapy for Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) has shifted from chemoimmunotherapy to targeted therapies, particularly BTK inhibitors and BCL2 inhibitors. These newer treatments have shown superior efficacy in terms of progression-free survival (PFS) compared to traditional chemoimmunotherapy.
keyPoints: Ibrutinib showed an 84% lower risk of progression or death compared to chlorambucil, with 59% of patients alive and progression-free at 7 years.
Acalabrutinib resulted in a 6-year PFS of 62%, and acalabrutinib plus obinutuzumab achieved a 4-year PFS of 78%.
Zanubrutinib demonstrated superior overall response rate and PFS compared to ibrutinib, with 2-year PFS at 85.5% in treatment-naïve patients.
Venetoclax plus obinutuzumab (VO) showed a median PFS of 76.2 months versus 36.4 months for chlorambucil plus obinutuzumab.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)
items.title: Choosing Between BTK Inhibitors: Efficacy and Safety
items.content: Second-generation BTK inhibitors, acalabrutinib and zanubrutinib, offer improved tolerability over the first-in-class agent ibrutinib. They are preferred in relapsed/refractory CLL due to lower rates of certain adverse events.
keyPoints: Acalabrutinib showed noninferior efficacy to ibrutinib with lower rates of atrial fibrillation, hypertension, myalgias/arthralgias, bruising, and skin/nail changes.
Zanubrutinib demonstrated superior overall response rate and PFS compared to ibrutinib in the ALPINE trial.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)
items.title: Venetoclax Therapy: Fixed-Duration vs. Indefinite BTK Inhibitors
items.content: Venetoclax, a BCL2 inhibitor, is used in combination with obinutuzumab for frontline CLL therapy and offers a fixed-duration treatment option. Unlike indefinite BTK inhibitors, traditional prognostic factors like IGHV mutational status are more relevant to PFS.
keyPoints: Venetoclax plus obinutuzumab (VO) showed superior median PFS of 76.2 months compared to chlorambucil plus obinutuzumab.
At 4 years, VO and IVO had superior PFS compared with chemoimmunotherapy (81.8% and 85.5%, respectively).
Genomic risk features are more relevant to PFS with fixed-duration VO therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)
items.title: When to Consider Targeted Therapy Combinations for CLL
items.content: Targeted therapy combinations, such as BTK and BCL2 inhibitors, are considered due to their synergy allowing fixed-duration therapies. The GLOW study demonstrated the efficacy of ibrutinib plus venetoclax (IV) with a 42-month progression-free survival (PFS) rate of 74.6% compared to 24.8% for chlorambucil plus obinutuzumab in older or unfit patients.
keyPoints: Synergy between BTK and BCL2 inhibition allows fixed-duration therapies.
GLOW study: 42-month PFS was 74.6% for IV vs. 24.8% for chlorambucil plus obinutuzumab in older or unfit patients.
IV is approved in Europe but not by the U.S. FDA.
Current studies compare IV and IVO efficacy.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)
items.title: Role of Chemoimmunotherapy in CLL
items.content: Chemoimmunotherapy, particularly FCR, can lead to durable responses over 20 years, especially in patients with mutated IGHV and favorable cytogenetics. However, its use is limited by toxicities such as myelosuppression and a 3–5% risk of fatal therapy-related myeloid neoplasm.
keyPoints: FCR can lead to durable responses over 20 years in patients with mutated IGHV and favorable cytogenetics.
Short-term toxicities include neutropenia and infection; long-term cytopenias are less common but possible.
3–5% risk of fatal therapy-related myeloid neoplasm with FCR.
E1912 study showed no difference in PFS or overall survival between FCR and IR for patients with mutated IGHV.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Optimal Sequencing of Targeted Agents in Relapsed CLL
items.content: In relapsed chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), the choice and sequence of targeted agents are crucial. Both BTK inhibitors followed by BCL2 inhibitors, or vice versa, are acceptable sequences. Retreatment with venetoclax after initial use is also viable.
keyPoints: BTK inhibitor then BCL2 inhibitor, or reverse, both acceptable sequences
Venetoclax retreatment possible; ORR 65%, median PFS ~2 years post-ibrutinib
BTK after venetoclax: ORR 84%, median PFS 32 months
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · THERAPY OF RELAPSED CLL

items.title: Noncovalent BTK Inhibitors for Resistant CLL
items.content: Pirtobrutinib, a noncovalent BTK inhibitor, is approved for patients with relapsed CLL resistant to both BTK and BCL2 inhibitors. It targets mutations like BTK C481S that confer resistance to covalent BTK inhibitors.
keyPoints: Pirtobrutinib approved by FDA for heavily pretreated high-risk patients
Effective against BTK C481S mutation
BRUIN study: ORR 82.2%, median PFS 19.6 months
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · THERAPY OF RELAPSED CLL

items.title: CAR-T Therapy in Relapsed CLL
items.content: CD19 CAR-T therapy, particularly lisocabtagene maraleucel (liso-cel), is being investigated for relapsed CLL. While initial trials showed limited success due to disease-associated immunosuppression, ongoing research aims to enhance efficacy.
keyPoints: CD19 CAR-T less successful in CLL compared to other B-cell malignancies
TRANSCEND CLL 004 trial: liso-cel ORR 43%, median PFS 11.9 months post-BTK and venetoclax
Current trials focus on optimizing CD19 CAR-T with additional agents or structural modifications
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · THERAPY OF RELAPSED CLL

items.subtopic: ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION
items.title: Importance of MRD in Assessing Long-term Outcomes in CLL Therapy
items.content: In chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), the detection and eradication of minimal residual disease (MRD) are crucial for predicting long-term survival outcomes. Complete tumor eradication, as evidenced by undetectable MRD, is linked to improved progression-free survival (PFS) and potentially curative results in specific therapies.
keyPoints: Complete eradication of MRD correlates with longer survival in various malignancies including CLL.
MRD negativity is defined as no malignant cells detected down to 1 CLL cell in $10^{4}$ leukocytes (0.01%).
Undetectable MRD after combination chemoimmunotherapy, especially FCR, is associated with long-term survival and potential cure.
In venetoclax-based regimens, undetectable MRD correlates with improved PFS.
MRD eradication does not serve as a meaningful endpoint with BTK inhibitors used alone.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION
items.title: Techniques and Sensitivity in Detecting MRD in CLL
items.content: The assessment of minimal residual disease (MRD) in chronic lymphocytic leukemia involves various methodologies, with bone marrow biopsies and flow cytometry being pivotal when initial tests do not detect the disease. Advanced techniques like next-generation sequencing can achieve higher sensitivity levels.
keyPoints: Bone marrow biopsy with flow cytometry is used to confirm complete remission (CR) if no disease is detected initially.
MRD negativity is defined as 1 CLL cell in $10^{4}$ leukocytes (0.01%) using standard methods.
Next-generation sequencing, such as ClonoSeq, can detect MRD at a sensitivity of 1 CLL cell in $10^{6}$ leukocytes (0.0001%).
Treatment strategies for MRD-relapsing CLL are currently under investigation in clinical trials.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION

items.question: What is the median age at diagnosis for chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) in the United States?
items.explanation: The median age at diagnosis of CLL is 71.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which population has the highest incidence of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)?
items.explanation: CLL is most common in Caucasians.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What was the age-adjusted incidence of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) per 100,000 people in the United States?
items.explanation: The age-adjusted incidence is 4.6/100,000 people.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What has been implicated as a service-connected condition for CLL in veterans?
items.explanation: CLL is a service-connected condition for those exposed to Agent Orange.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL
items.question: Which cell type is most commonly presumed to be the initiating cell in Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL)?
options: Mature lymphocyte, possibly memory B cells
items.explanation: The morphology and gene expression of CLL cells suggest a mature B cell origin.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL

items.subtopic: CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL
items.question: What does the presence of stereotyped BCRs in CLL indicate about its biology?
options: It supports the idea that CLL originates from mature lymphocytes.
It suggests a role for de-differentiation in CLL development.
It indicates a uniform antigen-binding characteristic across all CLL cases.
It contradicts the hypothesis of CLL originating from memory B cells.
items.explanation: The presence of stereotyped BCRs does not support the mature lymphocyte origin theory.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL

items.subtopic: CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL
items.question: Which cell has been proposed as a potential origin for CLL based on transplant studies in mice?
items.explanation: Transplant studies suggest that HSCs might be the originating cell for CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS
items.question: Which cytogenetic abnormality is associated with the poorest prognosis in chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)?
items.explanation: The del(17)(p13.1) results in loss of one allele of the tumor suppressor TP53 and is associated with the poorest prognosis in CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS
items.question: Which gene mutation is commonly found in IGHV-mutated chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) and associated with a more indolent prognosis?
items.explanation: Mutations in MYD88 tend to occur in IGHV-mutated CLL and are associated with a more indolent prognosis.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS
items.question: What is the most common cytogenetic abnormality in chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) and its impact on miRNA expression?
items.explanation: The most common cytogenetic abnormality in CLL is del(13)(q14.3), resulting in the loss of the miR15/16 cluster.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS
items.question: Which mutation is associated with lower sensitivity to CD20 antibody therapy and increased risk of transformation to aggressive diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) in CLL patients?
items.explanation: NOTCH1 mutations are associated with lower sensitivity to CD20 antibody therapy and increased risk of transformation to aggressive DLBCL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS

items.question: Which immune cell type is noted to have a qualitative defect similar to chronic antigen stimulation in CLL?
items.explanation: The passage states that within the CD4+ T-cell compartment, a qualitative defect similar to chronic antigen stimulation is noted.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · IMMUNOLOGY

items.question: What type of macrophage do M2 monocytes differentiate into in CLL?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that M2 monocytes differentiate into nurse-like cells in CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · IMMUNOLOGY

items.question: Which immunoglobulin subclass reduction is not associated with excess infection risk in CLL?
items.explanation: The passage states that isolated IgM reduction does not seem to be associated with excess infection risk.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · IMMUNOLOGY

items.question: What is the most common way CLL is diagnosed?
options: During routine blood work showing elevated lymphocyte count in asymptomatic individuals.
By identifying atypical phenotypes based on morphology, cytogenetics, or clinical presentation.
When clonal B-cell count exceeds 5 x 10^9/L.
items.explanation: CLL is most commonly diagnosed incidentally during routine blood work in asymptomatic individuals.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What immunophenotype markers are typically found in CLL?
options: CD19, CD20, CD22, CD23, CD200, and dim surface immunoglobulin.
CD3, CD4, CD8, and bright surface immunoglobulin.
CD5 only, with no other B-cell markers.
CD10, CD34, and bright surface immunoglobulin.
items.explanation: CLL typically shows the immunophenotype of CD19, CD20, CD22, CD23, CD200, CD5, and dim surface immunoglobulin.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSIS

items.question: When is no further workup needed to confirm the diagnosis of CLL?
options: When flow cytometry shows a clonal B-cell count ≥ 5 x 10^9/L.
If lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly are present.
When patients present with symptoms requiring early therapy.
items.explanation: No further workup is needed if flow cytometry shows a clonal B-cell count ≥ 5 x 10^9/L.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following best describes the progression rate to CLL in patients with high-count MBL?
items.explanation: Patients with high-count MBL progress to overt CLL at a rate of 1–2% per year.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · MONOCLONAL B-CELL LYMPHOCYTOSIS

items.question: What is the estimated prevalence of MBL in the general population?
items.explanation: Population-based studies have estimated the prevalence of MBL up to ~12% in the general population.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · MONOCLONAL B-CELL LYMPHOCYTOSIS

items.question: Which group has a higher frequency of MBL?
items.explanation: MBL is especially common in first-degree relatives of CLL patients, where the frequency is ~18%.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · MONOCLONAL B-CELL LYMPHOCYTOSIS

items.question: Which of the following vaccines is recommended for patients with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) despite limited vaccine response?
options: Influenza, COVID-19, varicella-zoster, pneumococcal pneumonia (Prevnar 20), and respiratory syncytial virus
Live attenuated vaccines such as MMR and oral polio
Only live vaccines are recommended for CLL patients
No vaccinations are recommended due to poor response
items.explanation: Vaccinations against influenza, COVID-19, varicella-zoster, pneumococcal pneumonia (Prevnar 20), and respiratory syncytial virus are recommended despite limited vaccine responses in CLL patients.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · COMPLICATIONS OF CLL | INFECTIONS

items.question: What is the approximate response rate of the recombinant zoster vaccine in previously untreated CLL patients?
items.explanation: The recombinant zoster vaccine has approximately a 60% response rate in previously untreated CLL patients.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · COMPLICATIONS OF CLL | INFECTIONS

items.question: Why should live vaccines be avoided in patients with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)?
options: They are ineffective due to poor immune response.
There is a small risk of viral reactivation in immunocompromised hosts.
Live vaccines can cause severe allergic reactions.
They increase the risk of secondary cancers.
items.explanation: Live vaccines should be avoided due to the small risk of viral reactivation with an immunocompromised host like those with CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · COMPLICATIONS OF CLL | INFECTIONS

items.question: Which type of cancer is most commonly seen at a higher rate in patients with CLL compared to the general population?
items.explanation: Skin cancers are particularly common, with a rate of 8- to 15-fold higher than in the general population.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · SECONDARY MALIGNANCIES | AUTOIMMUNE COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What is associated with an increased risk for secondary cancers after CLL-specific therapy?
items.explanation: Chemoimmunotherapy, particularly alkylator-containing regimens, and targeted therapies like BTK inhibitors are associated with increased risk for secondary cancers.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · SECONDARY MALIGNANCIES | AUTOIMMUNE COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as being associated with an excess risk of other cancers in CLL patients?
items.explanation: High LDH is associated with excess risk, not low LDH.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · SECONDARY MALIGNANCIES | AUTOIMMUNE COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What is the most common autoimmune complication in CLL?
items.explanation: The most common autoimmune complication is AIHA, which involves antibody-mediated destruction of RBCs.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · SECONDARY MALIGNANCIES | AUTOIMMUNE COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which lymphoma is most commonly associated with Richter's transformation in CLL?
items.explanation: Richter's transformation most commonly results in DLBCL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · RICHTER'S TRANSFORMATION

items.question: What is the preferred diagnostic method for Richter's transformation?
items.explanation: Excisional biopsy is the preferred mode of diagnosis for Richter's transformation.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · RICHTER'S TRANSFORMATION

items.question: Which SUV value on an $^{18}$FDG-PET/CT scan is very concerning for Richter's transformation?
items.explanation: SUVs ≥10 are very concerning for Richter's transformation.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · RICHTER'S TRANSFORMATION

items.question: In the workup of a new diagnosis of CLL, which test is typically not performed unless there are symptoms suggesting intraabdominal nodes out of proportion to peripheral nodes?
items.explanation: Imaging with a CT scan is usually not necessary unless there are symptoms and concern for intraabdominal nodes out of proportion to peripheral nodes.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · WORKUP AND STAGING | STAGING

items.question: Which staging system is more commonly used in the United States for CLL?
items.explanation: The Rai staging system is more commonly used in the United States for CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · WORKUP AND STAGING | STAGING

items.question: What is the primary reason for not undertaking bone marrow biopsy until therapy initiation in patients with CLL?
options: Unnecessary unless there are unexplained cytopenias
items.explanation: Bone marrow biopsy is not undertaken until therapy is initiated, except in cases of unexplained cytopenias.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · WORKUP AND STAGING | STAGING

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is a first-in-class covalent BTK inhibitor used in CLL therapy?
items.explanation: Ibrutinib is the first-in-class covalent BTK inhibitor used in CLL therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)
items.question: Which trial compared acalabrutinib with ibrutinib in high-risk relapsed CLL patients?
items.explanation: The ELEVATE-RR trial compared acalabrutinib with ibrutinib in high-risk relapsed CLL patients.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)
items.question: Which BTK inhibitor was shown to be superior to ibrutinib in terms of overall response rate and progression-free survival in the ALPINE trial?
items.explanation: Zanubrutinib was shown to be superior to ibrutinib in terms of overall response rate and progression-free survival in the ALPINE trial.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)
items.question: What is the fixed duration for venetoclax treatment when used in combination with obinutuzumab?
items.explanation: Venetoclax treatment, when used in combination with obinutuzumab, is administered for a fixed duration of 1 year.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)
items.question: Which combination of therapies showed a 42-month progression-free survival (PFS) rate of 74.6% in the GLOW study?
items.explanation: The GLOW study showed a 42-month PFS rate of 74.6% for the combination of ibrutinib plus venetoclax.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)
items.question: Which regimen has been shown to potentially cure CLL in a subset of patients with mutated IGHV and favorable cytogenetics?
items.explanation: Long-term follow-up of studies of FCR has demonstrated durable responses, potentially curing CLL in patients with mutated IGHV and favorable cytogenetics.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)
items.question: What is a significant long-term risk associated with the FCR regimen?
options: High incidence of acute renal failure
items.explanation: There is about a 3–5% risk of therapy-related myeloid neoplasm with the FCR regimen, which is almost always fatal.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: Which sequence of targeted agents is considered acceptable for treating relapsed CLL?
options: BTK inhibitor followed by BCL2 inhibitor or vice versa
PI3K inhibitors followed by BTK inhibitors
items.explanation: Both sequences of BTK inhibitor then BCL2 inhibitor and vice versa are acceptable.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · THERAPY OF RELAPSED CLL

items.question: What is the primary mechanism of acquired resistance to covalent BTK inhibitors in CLL?
options: Mutation at the binding site (predominantly BTK C481S)
items.explanation: The primary mechanism is acquisition of a mutation at the drug's binding site, predominantly BTK C481S.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · THERAPY OF RELAPSED CLL

items.question: What was the overall response rate (ORR) in the BRUIN study for pirtobrutinib?
items.explanation: The ORR was 82.2% in the BRUIN study for patients treated with pirtobrutinib.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · THERAPY OF RELAPSED CLL

items.question: What is a limitation of CD19 CAR-T therapy in treating CLL?
options: Lack of efficacy in B-cell malignancies
Inability to use alongside novel agents
items.explanation: CD19 CAR-T therapy has been less successful due to immunosuppression associated with CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · THERAPY OF RELAPSED CLL

items.subtopic: ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION
items.question: Which method is used to confirm complete remission (CR) in CLL if no disease is detected using physical examination, laboratory studies, and CT scans?
options: Bone marrow biopsies with flow cytometry
items.explanation: Bone marrow biopsies with flow cytometry are indicated to confirm CR if no disease is detected by other methods.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION
items.question: What level of MRD in the bone marrow indicates a patient is negative for minimal residual disease (MRD) in CLL?
items.explanation: A patient is considered negative for MRD if no malignant cells are detected down to a level of 1 CLL cell in 10^4 leukocytes (0.01%).
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION
items.question: Which treatment regimen is associated with potentially curative outcomes and long-term survival in a subset of patients?
options: Targeted therapy directed at BCL2 only
items.explanation: Eradication of MRD following FCR chemoimmunotherapy correlates with long-term survival and potentially cure in a subset of patients.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION

items.statement: The male-to-female ratio of CLL incidence becomes more even with increasing age.
items.explanation: Over the age of 80, the incidence is equal between men and women.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: CLL has been definitively linked to radiation exposure.
items.explanation: Unlike many other malignancies, CLL is not linked to radiation exposure.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL
items.statement: In CLL, approximately 60% of patients have IGHV regions that are ≥2% mutated from germline.
items.explanation: This indicates a more mature progenitor and is associated with an indolent disease course.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL

items.subtopic: CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL
items.statement: IGHV unmutated status in CLL is linked to a slower progression of the disease.
items.explanation: IGHV unmutated status is associated with rapid disease progression and shorter survival.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CELL OF ORIGIN | B-CELL RECEPTOR SIGNALING IN CLL

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS
items.statement: The presence of sole del(13)(q14.3) is associated with poor prognosis in CLL.
items.explanation: Sole del(13)(q14.3) is associated with more indolent disease and good response to traditional therapies.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS
items.statement: TP53 mutations are found in approximately 40% of CLL patients at later stages.
items.explanation: TP53 mutations occur up to 40% in later stages of CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS

items.statement: Hypogammaglobulinemia in CLL affects all subclasses of immunoglobulins.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that hypogammaglobulinemia affects all subclasses of immunoglobulins.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · IMMUNOLOGY

items.statement: CD8+ T cells in CLL exhibit a shift from Th1 to Th2 phenotype.
items.explanation: The passage specifies that it is the CD4+ T cells, not CD8+ T cells, that shift from a Th1 to a Th2 phenotype.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · IMMUNOLOGY

items.statement: The genetic and molecular features of SLL are identical to those of CLL.
items.explanation: SLL and CLL share the same genetic and molecular features.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: In cases with lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly, SLL is diagnosed instead of CLL.
items.explanation: SLL denotes a primarily tissue-based disease when there's lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: All cases of MBL progress to CLL.
items.explanation: Not all MBL progresses to CLL, analogous to MGUS in myeloma.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · MONOCLONAL B-CELL LYMPHOCYTOSIS

items.statement: Patients with low-count MBL have a high rate of progression to CLL.
items.explanation: Patients with low-count MBL have a negligible rate of progression to CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · MONOCLONAL B-CELL LYMPHOCYTOSIS

items.statement: Prophylactic intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) has been shown to improve survival in patients with chronic lymphocytic leukemia.
items.explanation: While IVIg reduces the number of minor or moderate bacterial infections, it has not been shown to improve survival.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · COMPLICATIONS OF CLL | INFECTIONS

items.statement: Vaccine efficacy in patients with chronic lymphocytic leukemia can be improved by repeated booster vaccinations.
items.explanation: Repeated booster vaccinations, vaccine adjuvants, and protein conjugation can improve vaccine efficacy in CLL patients.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · COMPLICATIONS OF CLL | INFECTIONS

items.statement: Specific recommendations for increased cancer screening in CLL patients are well-established.
items.explanation: There are no specific recommendations for increased cancer screening; age- and sex-appropriate screenings should be recommended.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · SECONDARY MALIGNANCIES | AUTOIMMUNE COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Autoimmune cytopenias in CLL always lead to a worse prognosis.
items.explanation: It is difficult to determine if autoimmune cytopenias lead to worse prognosis due to various complicating factors.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · SECONDARY MALIGNANCIES | AUTOIMMUNE COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Richter's transformation can result in both clonally related and unrelated lymphomas.
items.explanation: Lymphomas arising in the setting of CLL can be either clonally related or unrelated to the initial CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · RICHTER'S TRANSFORMATION

items.statement: Intensive chemotherapy is highly effective for most patients with Richter's transformation.
items.explanation: Intensive chemotherapy is ineffective for most patients and results in significant toxicity.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · RICHTER'S TRANSFORMATION

items.statement: Imaging with CT scan is routinely performed for all patients newly diagnosed with CLL.
items.explanation: CT scans are not usually necessary unless there are specific symptoms suggesting intraabdominal nodes out of proportion to peripheral nodes.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · WORKUP AND STAGING | STAGING

items.statement: The Binet system is more commonly used in the United States for staging CLL.
items.explanation: The Rai staging system, not the Binet system, is more commonly used in the United States.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · WORKUP AND STAGING | STAGING

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)
items.statement: Acalabrutinib and zanubrutinib are associated with higher rates of atrial fibrillation compared to ibrutinib.
items.explanation: Both acalabrutinib and zanubrutinib have lower rates of atrial fibrillation compared to ibrutinib.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)
items.statement: Traditional prognostic factors such as IGHV mutational status and FISH have less impact on outcome with covalent BTK inhibitors.
items.explanation: With the follow-up available from frontline studies of covalent BTK inhibitors, traditional prognostic factors have less impact on outcome.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)
items.statement: The GLOW study led to regulatory approval of IV in the United States.
items.explanation: Although the GLOW study led to regulatory approval of IV in Europe, it is not approved by the U.S. FDA.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)
items.statement: The E1912 study showed a difference in PFS or overall survival between FCR and IR for patients with mutated IGHV.
items.explanation: In the E1912 study, there was no difference in PFS or overall survival between FCR and IR for patients with mutated IGHV.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 2)

items.statement: PI3K inhibitors have high activity following both BTK and BCL2 inhibitors.
items.explanation: Activity of PI3K inhibitors is likely minimal following both BTK and BCL2 inhibitors.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · THERAPY OF RELAPSED CLL

items.statement: Reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) allogeneic transplants are suitable for patients up to ~75 years.
items.explanation: RIC allogeneic transplants have been successfully used in patients up to ~75 years.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · THERAPY OF RELAPSED CLL

items.subtopic: ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION
items.statement: Eradication of MRD is a meaningful endpoint with BTK inhibitors as monotherapy.
items.explanation: Eradication of MRD has not been shown to be a meaningful endpoint with BTK inhibitors as monotherapy.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION
items.statement: Undetectable MRD in blood or bone marrow is associated with improvement in PFS in venetoclax-based regimens.
items.explanation: Undetectable MRD is indeed associated with improved progression-free survival (PFS) in venetoclax-based regimens.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION

items.assertion: The prevalence of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) has increased over the past decades.
items.reason: This is due to improvements in therapy for CLL and survival of older patients from other medical ailments.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Specific genes conferring risk in familial CLL have been easily identified.
items.reason: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified ~30 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs).
items.explanation: The assertion is false; specific genes outside of specific families are difficult to identify despite GWAS findings.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS
items.assertion: Complex karyotype has a significant adverse impact on time to treatment and overall survival in CLL.
items.reason: Increasing complexity of cytogenetic abnormalities is more deleterious to response and survival.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS
items.assertion: Mutations in MYD88 are associated with poor prognosis in CLL.
items.reason: MYD88 mutations occur more frequently in IGHV unmutated disease and lead to aggressive disease progression.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; MYD88 mutations are actually associated with a more indolent prognosis. The reason is true but does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC ABNORMALITIES | GENE MUTATIONS AND MIR ALTERATIONS

items.assertion: Patients with SLL are managed identically to those with CLL.
items.reason: SLL and CLL have identical genetic and molecular features, leading to similar management strategies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) in CLL is often treated with glucocorticoids.
items.reason: Glucocorticoids are used for initial therapy due to their immunosuppressive effects.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why glucocorticoids are used.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · SECONDARY MALIGNANCIES | AUTOIMMUNE COMPLICATIONS

items.assertion: ITP in CLL is always treated by addressing the underlying CLL.
items.reason: Treatment of ITP often does not require treating the underlying CLL.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; treatment for ITP can be successful without treating the underlying CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · SECONDARY MALIGNANCIES | AUTOIMMUNE COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)
items.assertion: Venetoclax is administered continuously until disease progression.
items.reason: Venetoclax treatment is given for a fixed duration of 1 year.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because venetoclax is not administered continuously; it has a fixed duration.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)
items.assertion: Age and performance status were the only variables that predicted survival in the ELEVATE-TN trial.
items.reason: Traditional prognostic factors like IGHV mutational status and FISH had less impact on outcome with BTK inhibitors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why age and performance status were predictive.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ☑ CRITERIA FOR THE INITIATION OF THERAPY (part 1)

items.assertion: Pirtobrutinib has achieved FDA approval for use in relapsed CLL.
items.reason: It showed an overall response rate of 82.2% and median PFS of 19.6 months in a high-risk group.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the approval.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · THERAPY OF RELAPSED CLL

items.assertion: CAR-T therapy is less successful in CLL compared to other B-cell malignancies.
items.reason: CD19 CAR-T trials have shown high success rates in all B-cell malignancies.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as CD19 CAR-T therapy has not been highly successful in CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · THERAPY OF RELAPSED CLL

items.subtopic: ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION
items.assertion: Complete tumor eradication is associated with longer survival in various malignancies.
items.reason: In CLL, if no malignant cells are detected down to a level of 1 CLL cell in $10^4$ leukocytes (0.01%), the patient is said to be negative for MRD.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why complete tumor eradication leads to longer survival; it simply describes a criterion for MRD negativity in CLL.
items.reference: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia · ASSESSING RESPONSE TO THERAPY AND MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE IN CLL | CONCLUSION
